reply_context.text:
Why do language models love the "It's not just X. It's Y." construction so much?

 it's a definite tell and as someone who naturally trends towards that kind of rhetorical rhythm sometimes it's really annoying because I feel like I can't do it without having my humanity questioned anymore.

posts.text:
Think of it more like something in between "the model is thinking out loud" and "the model is stalling to make time to gather its thoughts.

The amount of computation that can go into any token is limited by the model depth. Phrases like this operate like buffers, pushing notes into the context.

posts.text: NB: commercial fertilizer formulations for industrial applications are unlikely to have pesticides/herbicides pre-mixed. That sort of thing is more common in the consumer market, e.g. residential lawn care.

reply_context.text: Fertilizer is often combined with organophosphate pesticides, which are indeed super toxic. But like, god forbid we wash our veggies before we eat them.

posts.text: I think my perspective was painted by the *consumer* fertilizer market, e.g. products for residential lawn care contain both fertilizer and herbicide. But apparently (and in retrospect, unsurprisingly), industrial farmers need to account for their specific conditions.

reply_context.text: Also, chatgpt says I'm just wrong and commercial fertilizers don't normally contain pesticides pre-mixed. So really I was just talking out of my ass.

posts.text: Also, chatgpt says I'm just wrong and commercial fertilizers don't normally contain pesticides pre-mixed. So really I was just talking out of my ass.

reply_context.text:
So both "fertilizer isn't toxic" is true and the phrase "cheap and toxic fertilizer" is valid. All I was going for was adding some nuance to the discussion.
